[*What Will A Man Give In Exchange For His Soul?*. by George D. Johnson, page 101](https://books.google.com/books?id=JmuuMa2uVTYC&dq=Jane+Elizabeth+%22Jennie%22+Faulding+Taylor&pg=PA101)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

**Herbert Hudson Taylor** (3 April 1861 – 6 June 1950), [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") to [China](/wiki/China "China"), author, speaker and eldest son of [James Hudson Taylor](/wiki/James_Hudson_Taylor "James Hudson Taylor"), founder of the [China Inland Mission](/wiki/China_Inland_Mission "China Inland Mission") and [Maria Jane Dyer](/wiki/Maria_Jane_Dyer "Maria Jane Dyer").[[1]](#cite_note-1) He served there for over 50 years – the last three as one of the prisoners of the Japanese at the [Weihsien](/wiki/Weihsien_Civilian_Assembly_Center "Weihsien Civilian Assembly Center") internment camp during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") along with [Eric Liddell](/wiki/Eric_Liddell "Eric Liddell") and 1500 others.

Herbert Taylor was four when his father founded the China Inland Mission. He had been born in [London](/wiki/London "London") during his parents’ first furlough in England together. At the time of his birth, they were living with his aunt and uncle Amelia and [Benjamin Broomhall](/wiki/Benjamin_Broomhall "Benjamin Broomhall") at 63 Westbourne Grove, in [Bayswater](/wiki/Bayswater "Bayswater"). In 1866 at the age of five Herbert and his parents, three siblings and sixteen other missionaries sailed to China aboard the [Lammermuir (clipper)](/wiki/Lammermuir_(clipper) "Lammermuir (clipper)") as part of the famous [Lammermuir Party](/wiki/Lammermuir_Party "Lammermuir Party"). During the four-month-long voyage the ship was nearly wrecked by two [typhoons](/wiki/Typhoons "Typhoons").

On arrival in China, the family adopted Chinese clothing and food and set off to find a place to establish a mission center in [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang"). On the way, Herbert almost drowned when he fell overboard into the [Grand Canal of China](/wiki/Grand_Canal_of_China "Grand Canal of China") during their travels. Another scare happened shortly afterward when he was bitten by a dog on the face. His sister, [Grace Dyer Taylor](/wiki/Grace_Dyer_Taylor "Grace Dyer Taylor") died of [meningitis](/wiki/Meningitis "Meningitis") near [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou") within the first year.[[2]](#cite_note-HCF-2) When he was six the family was nearly killed by a rioting mob during the [Yangzhou riot](/wiki/Yangzhou_riot "Yangzhou riot") in 1868. Finally in 1870 he was sent home with his surviving siblings with [Emily Blatchley](/wiki/Emily_Blatchley "Emily Blatchley") to live in London, separated from his parents. His mother died in China soon after they arrived home.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Like his father, he enrolled in the [Royal London Hospital](/wiki/Royal_London_Hospital "Royal London Hospital") medical college. After two years he decided to return to China to become one of the charter teachers at the newly founded China Inland Mission [Chefoo School](/wiki/Chefoo_School "Chefoo School") at ([Yantai](/wiki/Yantai "Yantai")). After marrying a fellow missionary, Jeanie Gray, they endured tumultuous years in China, including the [Boxer Rebellion](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion"), the fall of the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), the Warlord era, and the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") (1937).

In 1930 the China Inland Mission published "*[Ren Ch'eng-Yüan](/w/index.php?title=Ren_Ch%27eng-Y%C3%BCan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ren Ch'eng-Yüan (page does not exist)"), A Tamarisk Garden Blessed with Rain. Or the Autobiography of Pastor Ren. Translated and edited by Herbert Hudson Taylor & Marshall Broomhall.*"

At the age of 80 he was interned by the Japanese at the Weihsien or [Weifang](/wiki/Weifang "Weifang") concentration camp after [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") had entered the war in 1941. Everyone at Weifang were liberated in 1945 by American [paratroopers](/wiki/Paratroopers "Paratroopers").

Descendants of Herbert Hudson Taylor continued his full-time ministry today in the communities in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"). Grandson, the late Rev. [James Hudson Taylor III](/wiki/James_Hudson_Taylor_III "James Hudson Taylor III") (1929–2009) and his son Rev. James Hudson Taylor IV in Taiwan.
Another of Herbert Taylor’s grandchildren is [Mary Previte](/wiki/Mary_Previte "Mary Previte") who served in the [New Jersey General Assembly](/wiki/New_Jersey_General_Assembly "New Jersey General Assembly") representing the 6th legislative district from 1998 to 2006.
